corpus:skeleton.headline: Orient: what Determined found in the skeleton
corpus:skeleton.body: 17 files, 0 stubs, 1 hot symbol. capture is the heaviest entry point (13 outbound calls) — that's where the application's work happens. validate_entry and the ABC classes surface as roots even with no callers.
corpus:skeleton.what_to_notice: get_db (storage/db.py) is hot — 7 callers means the DB connection is a shared bottleneck. EntryProcessor and its 3 subclasses appear as roots because they have no callers yet, not because they're entry points.

frontier:direct:skeleton.headline: Direct mode: empty — and that's correct
frontier:direct:skeleton.body: Direct mode finds callers that call a stub (unimplemented function). The skeleton has 0 stubs, so there's nothing to show here. This means the codebase is implemented at this scale — nothing is definitively broken.
frontier:direct:skeleton.what_to_notice: Try Orphan mode next. 'Implemented but not broken' doesn't mean 'fully wired.' Orphan mode finds code that works but has no callers yet.

frontier:orphan:skeleton.headline: Orphan mode: validate_entry is written but unwired
frontier:orphan:skeleton.body: validate_entry exists in validator.py and works, but nothing calls it yet. 'Anticipatory' means it was written ahead of its callers — a common pattern when building incrementally.
frontier:orphan:skeleton.what_to_notice: The action is clear: wire validate_entry into the capture route. This is what Determined is for — not just finding bugs, but finding where the next connection belongs.

frontier:abc:skeleton.headline: ABC mode: no gaps — all overrides are in place
frontier:abc:skeleton.body: EntryProcessor has 3 subclasses (CleanupProcessor, DeduplicateProcessor, EnrichmentProcessor), and all have concrete overrides. The abstract interface is fully covered at skeleton scale.
frontier:abc:skeleton.what_to_notice: ABC gaps appear when a subclass exists but hasn't implemented a required method. An empty ABC view means the hierarchy is complete — check this again after adding new abstract methods.

topology:skeleton.headline: Topology: the whole corpus in one view
topology:skeleton.body: 0 stubs means nothing is broken. 2 orphaned-impl means there's code that's ready but not yet called. The action queue says it plainly: 'Write callers: orphaned-impl (2).'
topology:skeleton.what_to_notice: Chain positions (head/tail/middle) are empty at skeleton scale — not enough code to form a chain. The complete corpus shows these shapes clearly.

knowledge:skeleton.headline: Knowledge: empty by design on a fresh corpus
knowledge:skeleton.body: No distilled summaries, 0 design notes, 9/31 functions missing docstrings. This is the expected state — structural facts (entry points, hot, stubs) came from the ingest pass; the knowledge layer needs a separate pass.
knowledge:skeleton.what_to_notice: Next steps to populate: run extract_design_facts for structural findings, then ingest_design_docs with the path to examples/commonplace/docs/DESIGN.md to get layer rules and constraints.

editor:skeleton.headline: Editor: read any file in the corpus
editor:skeleton.body: Click a symbol anywhere in the tool to open it here, or type a file path directly. The editor shows docstrings, risk annotations, and lets you propose docstring fills.
editor:skeleton.what_to_notice: Try opening storage/db.py — it's the hot file (7 callers). See why get_db is the bottleneck.

call_tree:skeleton.headline: Call tree: trace who calls what
call_tree:skeleton.body: Enter a symbol name and trace up (callers) or down (callees). capture is the richest starting point — 13 outbound calls from the main entry point.
call_tree:skeleton.what_to_notice: Trace get_db upward to see all 7 callers. That's the dependency fan-in that makes it hot.

graph:skeleton.headline: Graph: the call graph as a visual map
graph:skeleton.body: Nodes are symbols, edges are calls. Hot symbols appear larger. The graph shows the skeleton's structure at a glance — capture at the top, get_db as the hub.
graph:skeleton.what_to_notice: Look for isolated clusters — those are the orphaned symbols that don't connect to the main graph yet.

chat:skeleton.headline: Ask: natural language queries over the corpus
chat:skeleton.body: Type any question about the codebase. Determined assembles relevant facts from the call graph, knowledge layer, and embeddings before answering. Works best on structural questions: who calls what, where does X live, what's hot.
chat:skeleton.what_to_notice: Try: 'What are the entry points?' or 'Who calls get_db?' The answer comes from the DB, not from an LLM hallucinating.

bag:skeleton.headline: Bag: your session context
bag:skeleton.body: The bag accumulates symbols and facts you've touched this session. It's context for the Ask bar — the more you explore, the better the answers get.
bag:skeleton.what_to_notice: After tracing a call tree or running a frontier query, check the bag to see what was accumulated.

dochealth:skeleton.headline: Doc health: 9 functions need docstrings
dochealth:skeleton.body: The skeleton has 22/31 functions documented. Doc health finds the gaps and can propose fills based on distilled summaries — once distillation has run.
dochealth:skeleton.what_to_notice: Distillation coverage is 0% on a fresh skeleton. Run describe_file or extract_design_facts first to get distilled summaries, then doc health proposals become useful.

build_queue:skeleton.headline: Build queue: proposed work items
build_queue:skeleton.body: When Determined proposes a fix, docstring fill, or gap closure, it lands here. The build queue is the hand-off between Determined's analysis and your next coding action.
build_queue:skeleton.what_to_notice: The queue is empty on a fresh skeleton — it fills as you run gap_analysis, docstring_health with propose=true, and similar tools.

corpus:complete.headline: Orient: the complete corpus
corpus:complete.body: 25 files, 2 intentional stubs, 16 orphaned implementations. Compare to the skeleton: 8 more files, chain positions now visible, and the orphan count jumped from 1 to 16 — mostly model layer and utility functions awaiting wiring.
corpus:complete.what_to_notice: The 2 remaining stubs (enrich_entry, semantic_search) are documented as intentional — 'STUB:' in the docstring. That's different from an accidental gap: the app runs, the stub just returns a fallback.

frontier:direct:complete.headline: Direct mode: 2 intentional stubs
frontier:direct:complete.body: enrich_entry (services/pipeline.py) and semantic_search (services/searcher.py) are stub_by_doc — they have real bodies but the docstring marks them incomplete. These are the 'what to implement next' targets.
frontier:direct:complete.what_to_notice: Both have functional fallbacks: semantic_search delegates to search(), enrich_entry returns early. The app works without them — they're improvements waiting to be built, not blockers.

frontier:orphan:complete.headline: Orphan mode: 16 orphaned implementations
frontier:orphan:complete.body: The orphan count jumped from 1 (skeleton) to 16 (complete). Most are model classes (Entry, Connection, Tag), HTML parser internals (extractor.py), and utility functions — all implemented but not yet called from routing code.
frontier:orphan:complete.what_to_notice: These are 'anticipatory' — built ahead of their callers. The exception: create_app is possibly-stranded (Flask factory, invisible to static analysis). The 15 others are real latent code waiting to be wired.

frontier:abc:complete.headline: ABC mode: hierarchy still complete
frontier:abc:complete.body: EntryProcessor's 3 subclasses all have concrete overrides in the complete corpus too. Adding 8 files didn't break the ABC contract — the hierarchy scaled cleanly.
frontier:abc:complete.what_to_notice: This is what good incremental extension looks like: new files added, interface coverage unchanged. ABC mode is the check that this held.

topology:complete.headline: Topology: chain positions now visible
topology:complete.body: Chain-tail and chain-head positions appear in the complete corpus — the call graph is dense enough to form real chains. The action queue now distinguishes 'implement now' (chain-tail stubs) from 'write callers' (orphaned-impl).
topology:complete.what_to_notice: In the skeleton, the queue was simple: write callers for 2 orphans. In the complete corpus it's prioritized: implement chain-tails first (they're blocking callers), then wire the remaining orphans.

knowledge:complete.headline: Knowledge: still empty on a fresh complete corpus
knowledge:complete.body: 42/64 functions missing docstrings, 0 design notes, 0 distilled summaries. The knowledge layer doesn't auto-populate — it needs explicit runs of extract_design_facts and ingest_design_docs.
knowledge:complete.what_to_notice: Running ingest_design_docs on examples/commonplace/docs/DESIGN.md is the first step to unlocking check_design_violations. Without design notes, violations can't be detected.

editor:complete.headline: Editor: 8 new files to explore
editor:complete.body: The complete corpus added models/ (Entry, Connection, Tag), routes/api.py, routes/browse.py, and utils/text.py and url.py. These are the wiring targets for the 16 orphaned implementations.
editor:complete.what_to_notice: Try opening models/entry.py — its validate() method is one of the orphaned impls. It's complete code waiting for a route to call it.

call_tree:complete.headline: Call tree: deeper chains to trace
call_tree:complete.body: The complete corpus has longer call chains. Trace capture downward to see the full pipeline: capture -> enrich_entry -> suggest_tags -> _call_llm (the stub chain).
call_tree:complete.what_to_notice: The stub chain from capture to _call_llm is exactly what Direct mode surfaces. Tracing it here shows the shape of what needs implementing — and where each function sits in the chain.

graph:complete.headline: Graph: the full corpus structure
graph:complete.body: 25 nodes, 8 more than the skeleton. The model layer (Entry, Connection, Tag) forms a cluster that connects to storage/queries.py but not yet to the routing layer — that's the orphan story made visual.
graph:complete.what_to_notice: Look for the isolated model cluster. Those nodes have edges to storage but no edges from routes — the missing wiring is visible as a gap in the graph.

chat:complete.headline: Ask: richer answers with more corpus
chat:complete.body: The complete corpus gives the Ask bar more to work with: 25 files, real chain positions, documented stubs. Try asking about specific stubs or orphaned implementations.
chat:complete.what_to_notice: Try: 'Why is semantic_search a stub?' or 'What calls enrich_entry?' The call graph and stub metadata make these answerable from the DB, not from guesswork.

bag:complete.headline: Bag: session context across a richer corpus
bag:complete.body: The bag accumulates symbols across your exploration. In the complete corpus, traces and frontier queries pull in more symbols — the bag gets richer faster.
bag:complete.what_to_notice: After tracing the stub chain (capture -> enrich_entry -> suggest_tags), check the bag. Those symbols plus their risk scores are context the Ask bar uses for follow-up questions.

dochealth:complete.headline: Doc health: 42 functions need docstrings
dochealth:complete.body: The complete corpus jumped from 9 to 42 missing docstrings. The model layer (Entry, Connection, Tag) and most utility functions have no documentation yet.
dochealth:complete.what_to_notice: Run with propose=true to generate fill candidates from distilled summaries. But distillation must run first — the complete corpus starts at 0% distilled, same as the skeleton.

build_queue:complete.headline: Build queue: where analysis becomes action
build_queue:complete.body: The complete corpus gives the build queue more material: gap proposals, docstring fills for 42 functions, and design violations once ingest_design_docs has run.
build_queue:complete.what_to_notice: Start with docstring_health and gap_analysis to populate the queue. Each accepted item becomes a concrete coding action — the queue is the bridge from 'what Determined found' to 'what you write next'.
